chidumo_masendeke.title: Zimbabwe's Real Life Prison Break — Chidumo and Masendeke
chidumo_masendeke.hook: Two men broke out of Zimbabwe's most secure prison — a place nobody had ever escaped from. And then they did it AGAIN.
key_facts: Stephen Chidhumo and Edgar Masendeke — convicted armed robbers, 16 counts each
First escape: overpowered warders at Mutimurefu Remand Prison in Masvingo, fled to Mozambique
Launched cross-border armed raids from Mozambique for 2 years
1997: Chidhumo caught in Mozambique, sentenced to 37 years at Chikurubi Maximum Security Prison
August 1997: Escaped Chikurubi — the ONLY successful escape ever from Zimbabwe's most secure prison
Escaped with fellow inmate Elias Chauke, Z$60,000 reward offered
Recaptured in Beira, Mozambique after 29 days
2002: Both Chidhumo and Masendeke were hanged
Chidhumo was approximately 30 years old at execution
Musician Kingbit immortalized them in song — they became folk heroes
His sister Phyllis: 'He might have been a criminal, but he was my brother. He was not only loving, but generous, too.'

nehanda.title: The Spirit Medium Who Cursed the Colonizers — And Her Curse Came True
nehanda.hook: In 1898, a woman was dragged to the gallows by the British. Her final words were a curse: 'My bones will rise again.' Eighty years later, they did.
key_facts: Nehanda Charwe Nyakasikana (c. 1840 – 27 April 1898)
Spirit medium (svikiro) who channeled the spirit of Nehanda
Led the First Chimurenga uprising in 1896 alongside Sekuru Kaguvi
Charged with ordering murder of Native Commissioner Henry Hawkins Pollard
Refused Christian conversion before execution — priest Father Richartz tried and failed
Hangman had difficulty killing her — legend says removing a tobacco pouch from her belt broke her spiritual protection
Famous last words: 'Mapfupa angu achamuka!' — 'My bones will rise again!'
Head was severed and shipped to England as a trophy — remains may still be there
Her prophecy seen as fulfilled by the Second Chimurenga (1964-1979) leading to independence

hyperinflation.title: The Country That Printed 100 Trillion Dollar Bills
hyperinflation.hook: Imagine being a trillionaire — and not being able to afford breakfast. In 2008, Zimbabwe made this everyone's reality.
key_facts: Peak inflation: 79,600,000,000% per month (November 2008)
Prices doubled every 24 hours
Z$100 trillion note = approximately 40 US cents
A loaf of bread cost Z$550 million (up to Z$10 billion on black market)
Z$100 billion bought just three eggs
Teachers earned trillions per month — worth about US$1
People carried cash in wheelbarrows
Shops updated prices hourly
80% unemployment
Caused by: violent land reform, Congo war spending, corruption, money printing
Currency abandoned April 2009, replaced with US dollar

great_zimbabwe.title: The African City Europeans Refused to Believe Existed
great_zimbabwe.hook: Europeans found a massive stone city in Africa — and spent 100 years lying about who built it. Because the truth destroyed their entire justification for colonialism.
key_facts: Largest ancient structure south of the Sahara
Built 11th-15th century by ancestors of the Shona people
Outer wall 820 feet in circumference, 36 feet high
Population 10,000-20,000 at peak
Chinese pottery, Persian bowls, Arab coins found — evidence of international trade
Karl Mauch (1871) claimed it was the Queen of Sheba's palace
Richard Hall (1902) hired by Cecil Rhodes, destroyed archaeological evidence
Peter Garlake proved Shona construction — Ian Smith's government exiled him
Rhodesian government censored all books attributing it to Africans
Zimbabwe named after the site: 'dzimba dza mabwe' = 'houses of stone'

gukurahundi.title: The Massacre Zimbabwe Tried to Erase From History
gukurahundi.hook: Between 1982 and 1987, an entire region of Zimbabwe was systematically terrorized. Up to 20,000 people were killed. And the man responsible became president.
key_facts: Gukurahundi = 'the early rain that washes away the chaff'
1982-1987 in Matabeleland and Midlands provinces
Fifth Brigade trained by North Korean military instructors
Targeted all Ndebele-speaking civilians indiscriminately
Cewale River Massacre: 62 young men and women shot, 7 survived
Bhalagwe concentration camp — victims thrown into disused mine shafts
Estimated 20,000 deaths (International Association of Genocide Scholars)
Chihambakwe Commission findings never released
Mugabe called it a 'moment of madness'
Emmerson Mnangagwa was Minister of State for National Security during the period
No senior official has ever been held responsible
